video_title: Crypto's Biggest Problem ft. Sam Newman of Defi.com
gist: Sam Newman of Defi.com argues DeFi's next maturity phase is a product problem, not an infrastructure one: he is building a self-custody consumer neobank for 'digitally native' users that bundles yield, a Visa spend card and tokenized RWAs, plus a portable onchain reputation score to unlock the missing piece of crypto finance — undercollateralized lending. He frames current low sentiment as a speculator problem while builders benefit from stablecoin payments, institutional inflows and TradFi acquiring crypto infrastructure.

insights.claim: Sam Newman argues crypto is entering a consolidation phase where traditional finance and web2 players are acquiring crypto infrastructure companies, and product is what converts inaccessible crypto concepts into real-world usable services.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Signals a wave of TradFi/web2 M&A of crypto-infra firms; watch for acquisition targets among rails/wallet/abstraction providers.
insights.quote: there's some big players coming in from traditional finance and the web two landscape and they are buying up and acquiring some of the crypto infrastructure companies

insights.claim: Newman says crypto is moving away from pure speculation toward solving real-world problems, driven primarily by stablecoin adoption in remittances and payments via crypto cards.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Real-world stablecoin payments/remittances are the growth vector; positions payments-utility crypto over speculative tokens.
insights.quote: crypto is now going through this kind of maturity cycle where it's moving away from just pure speculation into something that is solving problems for people

insights.claim: Defi.com is building a consumer-facing app targeting 'digitally native' rather than 'cryptonative' users, deliberately designed to feel closer to a fintech than a pure crypto DeFi app.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Product strategy bets on familiarity over ideology; the addressable user is existing fintech/online-banking users, not crypto insiders.
insights.quote: you can expect from defi.com to have a product that is going to feel closer to a fintech than maybe a a pure cryptonative defi app

insights.claim: Defi.com's app will bundle earning on funds through blue-chip DeFi yields, spending via a Visa card, easy token swaps, and investment in tokenized real-world assets in one non-custodial interface.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Full-stack neobank feature set (yield + card + swap + RWA) is the competitive surface for crypto-native fintechs.
insights.quote: They'll be able to earn on those funds through blue chip defy protocol yields. They'll also be able to spend their funds with a Visa card and access actual real world assets, goods and services.

insights.claim: Newman targets the roughly six billion people already onboarded to smartphones and online banking rather than trying to onboard all eight billion, deliberately excluding non-digitally-native users from the ideal target profile.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Contradicts the common 'onboard your grandma' pitch; disciplined targeting of a familiar-UX cohort reduces onboarding friction.
insights.quote: we are not trying to um explain every nuance to someone that's not used to using, for example, Revolute or Monzo or a fintech app

insights.claim: Newman contends crypto-first, self-custody-first companies hold a competitive edge over incumbent fintechs because they are built ground-up for crypto rails, analogous to how Amazon was built ground-up for e-commerce versus bookshops that later added online sales.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Frames Revolut/PayPal crypto features as bolt-ons; native-first builders may win the crypto-rails slice as it grows.
insights.quote: think of Amazon, for example, was built from the ground up for e-commerce, right? And we still have a load of um bookshops, for example, that are now selling online, but they're not as competitive um on the e-commerce side

insights.claim: Newman describes a pragmatic architecture with a decentralized non-custodial core and centralized fiat-interacting fringes where regulation applies, letting users move funds freely between the two sides.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: A hybrid model that concedes fiat touchpoints are centralized/regulated is a template for compliant self-custody products.
insights.quote: You you have a central core which is decentralized non-custodial and then on the fringes of that you have interactions with the fiat world where things are more centralized

insights.claim: Newman says self-custody is valued far more by users in jurisdictions with overreaching or corrupt government entities that may freeze funds than by users in most of Europe and the US.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Product-market fit for self-custody skews to emerging/high-risk jurisdictions; developed-market users treat it as under-the-hood.
insights.quote: they do care because they don't trust the relevant government entities to freeze their funds and not because they are doing anything wrong

insights.claim: Newman positions Defi.com as working alongside traditional banking rails in conjunction rather than replacing them, aiming to give users both self-custody control and the ability to interact with centralized entities.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Coexistence framing lowers regulatory/partnership friction versus a bank-disruption narrative.
insights.quote: I don't see us as replacing um you know traditional banking rails. I see us as working alongside them in conjunction.

insights.claim: Newman identifies undercollateralized lending as the biggest missing piece of DeFi, noting you cannot get a mortgage through DeFi unless you already hold all the money, for example in Bitcoin.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: The largest unaddressed TAM in onchain finance is credit; whoever cracks undercollateralized DeFi lending unlocks mortgages/auto finance.
insights.quote: if you want to get a mortgage through DeFi it's not really possible unless you've got all of the money for example in in Bitcoin

insights.claim: Defi.com is building a portable onchain reputation score based on a user's chain actions, akin to a FICO score, with selective disclosure to obtain better rates and eventually undercollateralized loans.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: An onchain credit-scoring/identity layer is the enabling primitive for DeFi credit markets; a potential moat if adopted cross-protocol.
insights.quote: we provide people with this reputation score based on the actions that they do on chain and they can selectively disclose certain aspects of their data and their score in order to get better rates

insights.claim: Newman frames the DeFi ID as a portable identity that carries reputation across protocols so users reap benefits when moving between them, with extended use cases like gated community membership requiring a minimum score.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Cross-protocol portable reputation could become an industry standard layer; watch for interoperability/adoption beyond one app.
insights.quote: It's a portable identity that if you um if you then move from one D5 protocol to another, you'd be able to hopefully reap the benefits of that

insights.claim: Newman says the undercollateralized credit product could be delivered either DeFi-first or via traditional credit lenders, but both routes require new partnerships, regulatory clarity, and maturation of the reputation score.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Signals partnerships and regulatory dependencies gate the timeline; near-term product will not include undercollateralized credit.
insights.quote: there's a number of different routes that it could take DeFi first or through traditional finance and traditional credit lenders both um would be feasible but the regulation and the um the the partnerships need to be developed

insights.claim: Newman argues users lose the privacy they implicitly had in traditional finance when moving onto public blockchains and ledgers, and Defi.com aims to restore it as a foundational architectural feature.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Privacy is reframed as a mass-adoption prerequisite, not a niche feature; a differentiator for onchain consumer products.
insights.quote: when they move to onchain to a public blockchain and a a public ledger so to speak they lose that privacy and we want to bring that back

insights.claim: Newman says portable DeFi identity and privacy are not mutually exclusive: a DeFi ID can receive funds like an ENS domain, but onchain balances and transactions cannot be traced back to it, with disclosure only to chosen counterparties.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Selective-disclosure identity design resolves the privacy-vs-reputation tension and is the technical crux investors should scrutinize.
insights.quote: You'll be able to see receive funds to that D5 ID the same way that you do with like an ENS domain, but you wouldn't be able to then look up my D5 ID and see that I've I've got a certain number of assets in there

insights.claim: Newman takes the contrarian view that crypto infrastructure is no longer the bottleneck, citing smart accounts, passkey signing, gas abstraction and chain abstraction as recently matured tools that abstract away self-custody complexity, much of which was not possible even 6-12 months ago.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: If infra is truly ready, execution/UX becomes the differentiator; undercuts the 'infra isn't ready' excuse used to explain low adoption.
insights.quote: now that we have smart accounts we have um path key signing we have gas abstraction and chain abstraction features as well it's maturing to a state where you can abstract away a lot of that complexity

insights.claim: Newman cites Hyperliquid as proof that teams should build their own infrastructure to achieve desired UX rather than blaming infrastructure gaps, and says Defi.com is both partnering with infra providers and building novel infrastructure to bridge gaps.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Vertical integration of infra where gaps exist is the winning pattern; 'infra isn't ready' is treated as an excuse.
insights.quote: I think of like Hyperlquid for example they they built their own infrastructure so that they could provide the user experience that they wanted to

insights.claim: Newman argues current low crypto sentiment reflects speculators and exchanges seeing lower volumes, while builders remain optimistic due to real-world adoption and institutional money flowing on chain.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Bifurcated outlook: speculative-volume businesses (exchanges) suffer while utility/adoption businesses grow; a stock-selection cue.
insights.quote: the sentiment is low and I I think it's low for maybe speculators and less so for builders

insights.claim: Newman calls stablecoin payments the big adoption driver, noting Visa and Mastercard now provide crypto-backed cards that let users spend crypto across millions of merchants.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Card-network integration is the concrete bridge from crypto to real-world spend; a measurable adoption metric to track.
insights.quote: partners like Visa and Mastercard are obviously providing cryptobacked cards now and you can suddenly spend your crypto and use your crypto across millions of merchants

insights.claim: Newman highlights emerging agentic crypto payments, including x402 where AI agents pay for API calls and infrastructure directly from a crypto wallet, as a fast-developing use case accelerating with AI.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Machine-to-machine crypto payments (x402/agentic) is an early frontier; a speculative but high-optionality theme to monitor.
insights.quote: x402 where where agents are paying for API calls and infrastructure directly from a crypto wallet

insights.claim: Newman points to Deel running stablecoin payroll and PayPal launching its own stablecoin as evidence that non-crypto companies are adopting crypto rails, validating a thesis he bet his career on since 2019.
insights.speaker: Sam Newman (guest, Defi.com)
insights.so_what: Non-crypto firms adopting crypto rails (payroll, branded stablecoins) is the proof-point for mainstream integration; a leading adoption indicator.
insights.quote: companies like Deal, for example, who who are running stable coin payroll. they're a HR payroll company, but they now have stable coin payroll products running
